justification: All pass criteria met: (1) 12-month growth projection calculated at ~9x/446K orders/day with detailed month-by-month table, (2) database identified as primary bottleneck — not API servers, (3) 85% disk utilization flagged as critical emergency with ~3-4 week runway, (4) index fix identified as highest ROI first step in Phase 1, (5) clear phased plan: indexes/archival → read replicas/cache → storage/partitioning → architectural evolution, (6) sharding explicitly deferred to Phase 4 with 'Do NOT implement sharding' in pitfalls, (7) trade-offs table covers replication lag and cache invalidation complexity.

observations.text: Growth projection math is precise and presented in a detailed month-by-month table with CPU and disk estimates
Disk emergency elevated beyond rubric expectations — called a 'ticking bomb' with 3-4 week runway and recommended immediate disk expansion THIS WEEK
Sharding explicitly deferred with strong language: 'premature and will consume all team bandwidth'
4-engineer team constraint woven throughout — monolith over microservices, sequential phases, feature velocity impact acknowledged
Index creation safety addressed: CONCURRENTLY flag, low-traffic window, replication lag monitoring
Exceptional depth: PgBouncer, materialized views, data archival, CQRS evaluation path — well beyond minimum requirements
Implementation guidance section gives Shaq actionable pitfalls and patterns to follow
Phase 1 combines indexing with archival and PgBouncer — rubric expects indexes as the clear singular first step; archival could arguably be Phase 2
Output is extremely long for a capacity planning exercise — could be more concise while hitting the same criteria

agent_output_excerpt:
# Capacity Analysis & Scaling Plan

## Executive Summary

Your system faces a **critical capacity crisis within 2-3 months**. At 20% month-over-month growth, you'll hit 446K orders/day by month 12 (~9x current load). The PostgreSQL database is the most urgent bottleneck — 85% disk utilization and full table scans on the highest-frequency queries will cause cascading failures well before that.
